root \ about \ projects + writings \ academic website

operator theory II: unbounded operators

Table of contents

General linear operators

Definition. Let $\calh$ be a Hilbert space. A linear operator on $\calh$ is a linear transformation $T:\Dom{T} \to \calh$ where $\Dom{T}$ is a vector subspace of $\calh$, called the domain of $T$. Though it may be the case that $\Dom{T} \neq \calh$, we nevertheless still write

\begin{equation}\notag T:\calh \to \calh, \end{equation}

leaving the domain of $T$ implicit. We write $\Ran{T}$ for the usual range of $T$.

  1. Let $S,T:\calh \to \calh$ be two linear operators. Then:

    • The linear operators $S$ and $T$ are said to be equal, written $S=T$, if $\Dom{S} = \Dom{T}$ and $Tv = Sv$ for all $v\in \Dom{S}$.

    • The sum of $S$ and $T$ is the linear operator \begin{equation}\notag S+T: \calh \to \calh, \quad v\mapsto Sv + Tv \end{equation} with $\Dom{(S+T)} = \Dom{S} \cap \Dom{T}$.

    • Let $\lambda \in \bbc$. The product of $c$ and $T$ is the linear operator \begin{equation}\notag \lambda T: \calh \to \calh, \quad v\mapsto \lambda Tv \end{equation} with $\Dom{(\lambda T)} = \Dom{T}$.

    • The composition of $S$ and $T$ is the linear operator \begin{equation}\notag ST : \calh \to \calh, \quad v\mapsto S(Tv), \end{equation} with $\Dom{ST} = T^{-1}(\Dom{S})$.

    • The operator $S$ is called an extension of $T$, written $S\supseteq T$, if \begin{equation}\notag \Dom{S} \supseteq \Dom{T} \quad \text{and} \quad Sv=Tv, \forall v\in \Dom{T}. \end{equation}

  2. Let $T:\calh \to \calh$ be a linear operator. Then $T$ is called …

    • … globally-defined if $\Dom{T} = \calh$; it is called densely-defined if $\Dom{T}$ is dense in $\calh$.

    • … bounded if there is a constant $C>0$ such that \begin{equation}\notag \norm{Tv} \leq C \norm{v}, \quad \forall v\in \Dom{T}. \end{equation} As usual, we write $\calb(\calh)$ for the set of all bounded linear operators on $\calh$.

    • … symmetric if \begin{equation}\notag \ang{Tv,w} = \ang{v,Tw}, \quad \forall v,w\in \Dom{A}. \end{equation}

    • … closed if its graph \begin{equation}\notag \Gamma(T) \defeq \{ (v,Tv)\in \calh \times \calh : v\in \Dom{T}\} \end{equation} is closed in the product topology on $\calh \times \calh$.

    • … closable if the closure of its graph $\Gamma(T)$ in $\calh \times \calh$ is the graph of a function.

Blah!

Definition. Let $S$ and $T$ be two linear operators on a Hilbert space $\calh$. We shall say $T$ is an extension of $S$, written $S\subseteq T$, provided $D(S) \subseteq D(T)$ and $S=T$ on $D(S)$.

Blah!

Definition. Let $T$ be a linear operator on a Hilbert space $\calh$. We shall say $T$ is boundedly invertible if there is a bounded linear opeartor $S$ on $\calh$ such that $TS=I$ and $ST \subseteq I$, where $I$ is the (globally-defined) identity operator on $\calh$.

Blah!

Theorem. If $T$ is a boundedly invertible linear operator on a Hilbert space $\calh$, then $T$ is bijective. Every bounded inverse of $T$ is globally-defined.

Let $S$ be a bounded inverse of $T$. Since $I$ is globally-defined, we have $TSv = v$ for all $v\in \calh$, which proves $T$ is surjective. By equating domains on both sides of $TS=I$, we also get

\begin{equation}\notag \calh = \Dom{I} = S^{-1}(\Dom{T}) \subseteq \Dom{S}, \end{equation}

which shows $S$ is globally-defined; hence

\begin{equation}\notag \Dom{ST} = T^{-1}(\Dom{S}) = \Dom{T}, \end{equation}

and so $ST \subseteq I$ implies $ST(v) = v$ for all $v\in \Dom{T}$. This proves $T$ is injective since it has a left-inverse. Q.E.D.

Multiplication operators

Let $L^2 = L^2(\bbr,\d q)$, where $\d q$ denotes the usual Lebesgue measure. We define the multiplication operator

\begin{equation}\notag Q: L^2 \to L^2 \quad \text{by setting} \quad (Q\psi)(q) = q\psi(q) \end{equation}

for all $\psi \in L^2$ and $q\in \bbc$. Interpreted literally, this actually isn’t well-defined! The problem is that there is no reason that $Q\psi \in L^2$ provided that $\psi \in L^2$. For example, the function

\begin{equation}\notag \psi: \bbr \to \bbr \quad \text{with} \quad \psi(q) = \begin{cases} 1/q & : q \notin (-1,1), \\ 0 & : q \in (-1,1) \end{cases} \end{equation}

is in $L^2$, but clearly $Q\psi \notin L^2$.

However, if we replace $L^2$ with the subspace

\begin{equation}\notag \Dom{Q} = \{ \psi \in L^2 : Q\psi \in L^2\}, \end{equation}

then we get a well-defined linear operator $Q:\Dom{Q} \to L^2$. One can even show that $\Dom{Q}$ is dense in $L^2$ by proving it contains $C_c(\bbr)$.

I now want to study the resolvents of $Q$.

Theorem. For each $\lambda \in \bbc$, define the linear operator

\begin{equation}\notag Q_\lambda: L^2 \to L^2 \quad \text{by} \quad (Q_\lambda\psi)(q) = (q - \lambda)\psi(q), \end{equation}

with $\Dom{Q_\lambda} = \Dom{Q}$.

  1. The linear operator $Q_\lambda$ is injective.

  2. If $\lambda \notin \bbr$, then $Q_\lambda$ is boundedly invertible.

  3. If $\lambda \in \bbr$, then $Q_\lambda$ is not surjective, but as dense range.

(1.): Suppose that $\psi\in \Dom{Q}$ is in the kernel of $Q_\lambda$, so that

\begin{equation}\notag (q-\lambda)\psi(q) = 0 \end{equation}

for almost all $q\in \bbr$. But then we have $\psi(q)=0$ for almost all $q$, and hence $\psi =0$ in $L^2$. This proves $\psi$ is injective.

(2.): Define the linear operator

\begin{equation}\notag S_\lambda: L^2 \to L^2, \quad (S_\lambda\varphi)(q) = \frac{\varphi(q)}{q-\lambda}. \end{equation}

This operator is globally-defined; in fact, we actually have $\Ran{S_\lambda} \subseteq \Dom{Q_\lambda}$. To prove this, let $\varphi\in L^2$ and note that

\begin{equation}\notag \int_\bbr q^2 \left| \frac{\varphi(q)}{q-\lambda} \right|^2 \ \d q \leq C \int_\bbr |\varphi(q)|^2 \ \d q < \infty \end{equation}

where $C$ is a positive constant bounding the function $q^2/|q-\lambda|^2$. Moreover, $S_\lambda$ is also bounded, for we have

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{S_\lambda\varphi}^2 = \int_\bbr \frac{|\varphi(q)|^2}{|q-\lambda|^2} \ \d q \leq \frac{1}{|\imag{\lambda}|^2} \norm{\varphi}^2 \end{equation}

since

\begin{equation}\notag |\imag{\lambda}| = |\real{\lambda} - \lambda | \leq |q-\lambda| \end{equation}

for all $q\in \bbr$. I will leave it to the reader to check that

\begin{equation}\notag Q_\lambda S_\lambda = I \quad \text{and} \quad S_\lambda Q_\lambda \subseteq I, \end{equation}

which includes checking that the domain of $Q_\lambda S_\lambda$ is all of $L^2$.

(3.): The function $\varphi(q) = e^{-q^2}$ is in $L^2$, but it is not in the range of $Q_\lambda$. Indeed, if there were a function $\psi \in D(Q)$ such that $Q_\lambda \psi = \varphi$, then we must have

\begin{equation}\notag \psi(q) = \frac{e^{-q^2}}{q-\lambda} \end{equation}

for almost all $q\in \bbr$. But $\psi \notin L^2$ if $\lambda \in \bbr$.

We now argue that the range space $\Ran{Q_\lambda}$ is dense in $L^2$. I shall prove this by showing the orthogonal complement is zero:

\begin{equation}\label{comp-eqn} (\Ran{Q_\lambda})^\perp=0. \end{equation}

As a preliminary step, we let $K$ be a compact subset of $\bbr$, let $\chi_K$ its characteristic function, and let $\varphi\in L^2$. Notice that the functions

\begin{equation}\label{huh-eqn} q\mapsto \frac{\varphi(q)\chi_K(q)}{q-\lambda} \end{equation}

are contained in $\Dom{Q_\lambda}$, and $\varphi\chi_K$ is the image of \eqref{huh-eqn} under $Q_\lambda$. Thus, the range space $\Ran{Q_\lambda}$ contains all functions of the form $\varphi\chi_K$ where $\varphi \in L^2$ and $K$ is a compact subset of $\bbr$ not containing $\lambda$.

Therefore, if $\varphi \in (\Ran{Q_\lambda})^\perp$, then we have

\begin{equation}\notag \int_K |\varphi(q)|^2 \ \d q = \int_\bbr \varphi(q) \overline{\varphi(q)} \chi_K(q) \ \d q = \ang{\varphi,\varphi\chi_K} = 0 \end{equation}

for all compact sets $K$ not containing $\lambda$. For each integer $n\geq1$, we define

\begin{equation}\notag K_n = [-n,\lambda - 1/n] \cup [ \lambda + 1/n,n]. \end{equation}

Since $|\varphi \chi_{K_n}|^2 \to |\varphi|^2$ pointwise almost everywhere, $|\varphi \chi_{K_n}|^2 \leq |\varphi|^2$, and $|\varphi|^2 \in L^1$, by dominated convergence we have

\begin{equation}\notag \norm{\varphi}^2 = \int_\bbr |\varphi(q)|^2 \ \d q = \lim_{n\to \infty} \int_\bbr |\varphi(q) \chi_{K_n}(q) |^2 \ \d q = \lim_{n\to \infty} \int_{K_n} |\varphi(q)|^2 \ \d q = 0. \end{equation}

Thus, $\varphi=0$ in $L^2$, establishing the desired result \eqref{comp-eqn}. Q.E.D.

Spectra of general linear operators

Definition. Let $T$ be a linear operator on a Hilbert space $\calh$ with domain. For each $\lambda \in \bbc$, we define the linear operator

\begin{equation}\notag T-\lambda: \calh \to \calh, \quad (T-\lambda)v = Tv - \lambda v, \end{equation}

with $\Dom{(T-\lambda)} = \Dom{T}$. The resolvent set of $T$, denoted $\rho(T)$, is the set of all $\lambda \in \bbc$ for which $T-\lambda$ is boundedly invertible. The spectrum of $T$, denoted $\sigma(T)$, is the complement $\bbc \smallsetminus \rho(T)$.

Blah!

Definition. Let $T$ be a linear operator on a Hilbert space $\calh$ with spectrum $\sigma(T)$. Then $\sigma(T)$ decomposes as a disjoint union

\begin{equation}\notag \sigma(T) = \sigma_p(T) \cup \sigma_c(T) \cup \sigma_r(T) \end{equation}

where:

  1. The set $\sigma_p(T)$, called the point spectrum of $T$, consists of those $\lambda$ such that $T-\lambda$ is not injective. The numbers in $\sigma_p(T)$ are called eigenvalues of $T$.

  2. The set $\sigma_c(T)$, called the continuous spectrum of $T$, consists of those $\lambda$ such that $T - \lambda$ is injective with dense range, but for which $T-\lambda$ is not surjective.

  3. The set $\sigma_r(T)$, called the residual spectrum of $T$, is the complement in $\sigma(T)$ of $\sigma_p(T) \cup \sigma_c(T)$.
